problem_summary: 2023 MCM/ICM Problem E (sustainability / light pollution). The task: build a metric to quantify the light-pollution risk level of a location, apply it to four distinct location types (urban, suburban, rural, protected land), propose intervention strategies, model their potential effect, and pick/communicate the best one. This paper answers it with a weighted composite index (LPI) built on 10 indicators grouped into Light/Society/Nature, classifies risk into 4 grades via fuzzy clustering, then uses correlation analysis + BP neural networks to simulate how interventions propagate through the indicators and change the LPI for two representative cities, finishing with a public-facing flyer.
models_used: Light-Society-Nature (LSN) composite evaluation model: LPI = 100·(ωL·L + ωS·S + ωN·N), a weighted-sum index over 10 second-level indicators in 3 groups (Light: artificial light intensity, density, sky brightness, glare; Society: population, development/GDP, propaganda & education; Nature: climate, geography, biodiversity)

Combination weighting = average of three weight vectors: Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP, subjective, with consistency check) + Entropy Weight Method (EWM, objective, information-entropy based) + Coefficient of Variation Method (CVM, objective). Final ωj = (ωj1+ωj2+ωj3)/3
Min-max normalization with separate positive/negative-indicator formulas to remove dimensionality
Fuzzy cluster analysis to partition 55 locations' LPI into 4 risk grades (Heavy/Moderate/Minimal/Unpolluted; thresholds <40 / 40-60 / 60-80 / >80)

PIA-NN (Potential Impact Analysis–Neural Network) model: Spearman rank correlation (chosen because only sky brightness passed a normality test) to find direct→indirect indicator links, then three BP neural networks (built with MATLAB Neural Fitting / nftool) mapping each intervention's direct indicator to its correlated indirect indicators
Custom domain formulas: glare L4 = -8·(L1/S_location)·lg(0.25/L3); climate N1 = trp'+wet'; geography N2 = h'+t'; biodiversity N3 = S_green/S_location + C_wild/S_green
key_techniques: Triangulating weights from three independent methods (AHP+EWM+CVM) and averaging them to neutralize the subjectivity of AHP and the data-sensitivity of the objective methods — a defensible hedge against any single weighting being criticized
Two-level (first/second-level) indicator hierarchy that maps cleanly onto the problem's Light/Society/Nature framing and makes the index interpretable

Grounding every indicator in a named, reproducible public data source (Light Pollution Map, national statistical yearbooks, NOAA, Google Trends for the 'propaganda/education' proxy, National Forestry Administration)
Using Google Trends search volume as a creative quantitative proxy for the otherwise-soft 'publicity and education' indicator
Normality testing before choosing a correlation measure (Spearman over Pearson) — a small but judge-pleasing piece of statistical rigor

Reframing intervention strategies as perturbations to specific indicators, then propagating them through learned NN relationships to recompute LPI — connecting Task 3/4 back to the Task 1 model instead of treating them separately
Controlled comparison design: fixing all strategies to the same ±10% perturbation so strategy effectiveness is compared on equal footing
Sensitivity analysis by re-training the NN with an indicator removed and showing before/after predictions nearly coincide, used as a robustness argument

Validation by face-checking the four computed LPIs (NYC=35.55 GradeI, Bellevue=41.33 GradeII, Sedona=76.94 GradeIII, Yellowstone=84.18 GradeIV) against real-world expectations, including Yellowstone's Dark Sky Park status
why_it_won: "From a judge's view it nails the full arc of Problem E: it produces a single interpretable number (LPI) with a clean 3-pillar story, applies it convincingly to all four required location types with results that match intuition, and — crucially — it closes the loop by feeding intervention strategies back through the same indicator system rather than answering each sub-task in isolation. The combination-weighting (AHP+EWM+CVM) preempts the obvious 'your weights are arbitrary' critique, the Spearman-after-normality-test detail signals statistical maturity, and the sensitivity analysis gives a concrete robustness claim. It is methodologically broad (MCDA + clustering + correlation + neural nets) yet stays coherent and readable, and it delivers the required flyer with a crisp 3-step message. Triangulation, real named data, intuition-matching validation, and a tight narrative are exactly what separates an O from a Finalist."
section_highlights: "Summary front-loads every numeric result (the four LPIs and their grades, the winning strategy) so a judge gets the whole answer in 30 seconds. A whole-paper flowchart (Figure 1) maps the five tasks and the AHP/EWM/CVM→Combine pipeline up front. Assumptions are paired with explicit justifications. A notation table precedes the math. Each of the four case locations gets its own data table + a paragraph of real-world reasoning tying the number to local facts (NYC nightlife/skyglow, Yellowstone's Dark Sky efforts). The weight pie chart (Figure 4) makes the dominance of Light (≈73%) and artificial-light-intensity visible at a glance. Strengths/Weaknesses are candid (admits 55-location database is small, LPI ranges fuzzy, missing data). The flyer translates the technical winner (Strategy I = reduce artificial light at the source) into a 3-step public message ('light only if/when/where needed; prefer warm/amber LED; downlighting shielded')."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Several core formulas are physically ad hoc and unjustified: the glare formula L4 = -8·(L1/S)·lg(0.25/L3) and biodiversity N3 are presented without derivation or citation for the constants; 'artificial light density L2 = L1/S_location' is a definitional shortcut, not a real density
The LPI is inverted (higher LPI = LESS pollution), which is counterintuitive for a 'pollution index' and risks confusing readers despite the stated assumption

Fuzzy clustering yields 4 classes but the paper itself admits the LPI value ranges per class are not clearly determined — the <40/40-60/... thresholds look hand-set rather than derived from the clustering

The PIA-NN figures are suspicious: the embedded data tables show suspiciously smooth, near-linear/monotone synthetic-looking trends (e.g. Strategy II indicators flattening to constants), suggesting the NN is overfit or the curves are illustrative rather than genuine predictions from 55 points
Training BP neural networks on only 55 locations to learn multi-output indicator relationships is statistically thin and prone to overfitting; no train/test split or error metric is reported
The judgment matrices for AHP are omitted ('limited space'), so the most subjective step is unauditable

The ±10% perturbation comparison conflates effect-size across strategies that act on differently-scaled indicators, so 'Strategy I is best' partly reflects Light's huge ≈73% weight by construction, not an independent finding
Causal claims (vegetation absorbs light pollution; more biodiversity lowers sky brightness) are asserted from correlation, risking reverse-causation
reusable_patterns: Composite-index recipe for any 'evaluate the risk/quality level of X' prompt: pick a memorable 3-pillar grouping, choose ~10 publicly-sourced indicators, normalize, weight, sum to one branded index (here LPI), then bucket into named grades via clustering
Combination-weighting triangulation (AHP + EWM + CVM, then average) as a reusable, criticism-proof way to set weights when no ground truth exists
Always validate a constructed index by computing it on a few well-known cases and showing the ranking matches common sense — cheap, persuasive, and judge-friendly

Treat interventions as perturbations (±k%) to specific model indicators and propagate them through learned relationships back into the same index to score strategies — a clean way to unify 'build a metric' and 'recommend an action' sub-tasks
Run a normality test before picking Pearson vs Spearman, and state it — low-cost rigor signal
Do sensitivity analysis by dropping/altering one input and overlaying before/after curves to argue robustness
Use a search-trend or other behavioral-data proxy to quantify an inherently qualitative indicator (awareness/education)
Front-load all numeric answers in the Summary and include a single whole-paper task-flow diagram so judges can navigate quickly
End a communication-oriented (Problem E) paper with the actual requested deliverable (flyer/letter) that distills the technical winner into 2-3 plain-language actions
